question: How close are the games -- nailbiters or blowouts?

headline.text: About 1 in 7 games (13%) comes down to one possession, and roughly a third (32%) are decided by 6 points or fewer. But the league is not all nailbiters: 44% of games end in double digits and 15% are 20-point blowouts, with a typical (median) margin of 9. And games are NOT getting closer over the past decade -- margins are flat to slightly wider, just noisy from year to year. The Las Vegas Aces play the most lopsided games on average (13.1 points) while the Atlanta Dream play the closest (10.5).

trend_summary.read: No meaningful trend toward closer games. Average margins are flat to slightly wider across the seasons and swing from year to year; the share of one-possession finishes has stayed near 11-17% throughout.

validation.method: final-result margin vs the same margin re-summed from per-player box scores

definitions.margin: the final points gap of a game (winning score minus losing score); always 1 or more
definitions.one_possession: a game decided by 3 points or fewer
definitions.close: a game decided by 6 points or fewer
definitions.double_digit: a game decided by 11 points or more
definitions.blowout: a game decided by 20 points or more
definitions.team_tilt: the average final margin of the games a team plays in, counting its own wins and losses; a high number means that team is usually in a lopsided game, a low number means close games

sources: per-game final results (2015-2026) -- the exact final scores
per-player box scores -- summed independently as an honesty cross-check of the margins
provenance.method: empirical; the final margin is exact (winning minus losing score), buckets partition every game so shares total 100, and every margin is cross-checked against an independent box-score reconstruction that agrees 98% of the time; per-cell counts stated
caveats: Only completed games with two real final scores are counted; scheduled and unplayed games are excluded. The current-season sample is partial and grows as games are played.
The season trend is weak and noisy: the year-to-year swing is larger than the decade-long drift, so the honest read is 'no meaningful move toward closer games', not a firm direction.

Team tilt is descriptive, not a quality rating -- a high average margin can mean a team wins big (a strong team) or loses big (a weak team); it only says its games tend to be lopsided. Only teams with at least 100 games appear, and relocated franchises are shown under their historical code (San Antonio, Tulsa).
Team-name aliases (relocations/expansion) are normalized to one canonical code per franchise; expansion clubs with small samples are held off the leaderboard.

index_row.title: How close are the games?
index_row.one_line: About 1 in 7 games (13%) is a one-possession nailbiter and ~32% are decided by 6 or fewer -- but 15% are 20-point blowouts, and games are not getting closer.
index_row.headline: 13% of games come down to one possession; 15% are 20-point blowouts.
index_row.blurb: The full spread of final margins -- nailbiters versus blowouts, the biggest routs on record, whether games are getting closer, and which teams play the tightest and most lopsided games.
index_row.question: How close are the games, and are they getting closer?
